items.question: Define variance in the bias–variance decomposition.
items.answer: Variance is Var(ŷ(x)) = E_D[(ŷ(x) − E_D[ŷ(x)])²]: the expected squared deviation of predictions from the mean prediction across training datasets D. It measures how much predictions change when you retrain on different samples.

items.question: How do you recognize high variance in train vs validation metrics?
items.answer: Training performance is much better than validation (large gap), and retraining on new data shifts predictions or fold scores noticeably. Learning curves show training score high while validation lags and may improve with more data.

items.question: Name three techniques to reduce model variance and one tradeoff each.
items.answer: Regularization (L2/dropout) adds bias but stabilizes weights. More training data lowers variance but costs collection. Bagging/random forests average unstable learners—more compute and less interpretability. Simpler models (shallower trees) raise bias if taken too far.

items.question: Write the squared-error decomposition and identify the irreducible term.
items.answer: E[(y − ŷ)²] = Bias²(ŷ) + Var(ŷ) + Var(ε|x). The last term is irreducible noise in y given x; bias and variance are reducible through model choice, data, and regularization.
